coredata.dc:title: Sub-30-Seconds ultrafast intelligent detection of glutathione using machine learning-guided handheld sensing platform based on mercury ion-mediated ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Jianyang
Shi, Lihong
Chang, Dan
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, accurate, and in-field detection of glutathione (GSH) is indispensable for food safety, medical diagnosis, and environmental monitoring. However, most conventional approaches typically require the use of expensive laboratory-based techniques and trained personnel. Herein, combined with a machine learning algorithm, a portable handheld sensor based on mercury ion (Hg2+)-mediated ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots (D-CDs) is first constructed for ultrafast detection of GSH. The intelligent system utilizes a smartphone with a self-programming applet as a real-time result-processing terminal, which greatly improves the detection accuracy and efficiency. Interestingly, orange emission of D-CDs gradually decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentrations, while green emission shows an obvious enhancement, resulting in a distinct color shift from orange to green. Subsequent addition of GSH restores the fluorescence of D-CDs@Hg2+ accompanied by a noticeable color transition from green to orange. More importantly, the proposed method realizes on-site monitoring of GSH with the detection limit of 1.84 μM. The application of machine learning technology on automated handheld sensors shows its potential for sample-to-answer detection, providing a valuable and efficient tool for rapid on-site chemical analysis and intelligent point-of-care diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Machine learning
Handheld sensor
Hg2+
GSH

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric sensing of Hg2+ and GSH
Ml-assisted handheld sensor for on-site testing
Detection of Hg2+ and GSH in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090 and 21874087).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic structure (A − B) and actual disassembly (C) of 3D-printed accessory.

(A) TEM image of D-CDs (scale bar is 20 nm), Inset shows the statistical particle size distribution. (B) HRTEM image of D-CDs (scale bar is 2 nm). (C) AFM topographic image of D-CDs (Inset is the height profile along a white line). (D) Height distribution of D-CDs in the whole AFM topographic image. (E) XPS full-survey of D-CDs. (F − H) High-resolution XPS of C1s, N1s, and O1s of D-CDs. (I) FTIR spectrum of D-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation-emission spectra of D-CDs. The insets are the pictures of D-CDs under sunlight and UV light, respectively. (B) FL spectra of D-CDs under different excited wavelengths (370 nm − 450 nm). (C) FL spectra of D-CDs with different concentrations of Hg2+. (D) Functional relationship between I616/I550 and Hg2+ concentration. (E) FL spectra of D-CDs@Hg2+ with different concentrations of GSH. (F) Functional relationship between I616/I550 and GSH concentration.

(A − B) TEM image and particle size distribution of D-CDs@Hg2+ (scale bar is 50 nm). (C − D) TEM image and particle size distribution of D-CDs@Hg2+@GSH (scale bar is 20 nm). (E − F) AFM topographic image (Inset is the height distribution along a white line) and height distribution of D-CDs@Hg2+. (G − H) AFM topographic image (Inset is the height distribution along a white line) and height distribution of D-CDs@Hg2+@GSH. (I) FT-IR spectra of D-CDs, D-CDs@Hg2+, and D-CDs@Hg2+@GSH. (J) FL lifetimes of D-CDs, D-CDs@Hg2+, and D-CDs@Hg2+@GSH.
Reaction mechanism of D-CDs responding to Hg2+ and GSH.
Steps for analysing GSH concentrations using custom-built WeChat mini-program.
Diagram of intelligent visualization assay of Hg2+ and GSH based on handheld sensor.

head.ce:title: Sub-30-Seconds ultrafast intelligent detection of glutathione using machine learning-guided handheld sensing platform based on mercury ion-mediated ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, accurate, and in-field detection of glutathione (GSH) is indispensable for food safety, medical diagnosis, and environmental monitoring. However, most conventional approaches typically require the use of expensive laboratory-based techniques and trained personnel. Herein, combined with a machine learning algorithm, a portable handheld sensor based on mercury ion (Hg2+)-mediated ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots (D-CDs) is first constructed for ultrafast detection of GSH. The intelligent system utilizes a smartphone with a self-programming applet as a real-time result-processing terminal, which greatly improves the detection accuracy and efficiency. Interestingly, orange emission of D-CDs gradually decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentrations, while green emission shows an obvious enhancement, resulting in a distinct color shift from orange to green. Subsequent addition of GSH restores the fluorescence of D-CDs@Hg2+ accompanied by a noticeable color transition from green to orange. More importantly, the proposed method realizes on-site monitoring of GSH with the detection limit of 1.84 μM. The application of machine learning technology on automated handheld sensors shows its potential for sample-to-answer detection, providing a valuable and efficient tool for rapid on-site chemical analysis and intelligent point-of-care diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Integrated handheld analytical platforms play a critical role in disease screening, food safety inspection, and environmental pollutant monitoring, showing promise in resource-limited areas without centralized instrumentation [1]. These platforms, which generally comprise smartphones, chemical sensors, and miniaturized devices with optical and electrochemical components, are recognized as powerful tools owing to their advantages of simple operation, rapid analysis, and high portability [2]. At present, numerous strategies for the detection of various targets have been reported. Li et al. designed a portable smartphone-integrated handheld device in which a DVD grating serves as the miniature diffraction component for quantification of neurotoxin [3]. Zhang et al. proposed a sensing system combining a smartphone with up-conversional test strips for rapid analysis of thiram [4]. However, these methods are difficult to achieve satisfactory detection accuracy and efficiency due to the need for manual data processing, which are not suitable for on-site applications. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop a simple and efficient strategy for intelligent quantification of targets.

Artificial intelligence (AI) refers to machine intelligence, in which computer systems can generate and analyze massive amounts of experimental data and use these learnings to solve associated tasks without human intervention [5]. Machine learning (ML), as an important branch of AI technology, has become highly sought-after in the fields of structure-based virtual screening, drug monitoring, peptide synthesis as well as chemical sensing [6–8]. It is worth noting that the ML-driven object detection approach represented by the YOLOv3 model demonstrates exceptional capabilities in terms of rapid data processing, strong adaptability, and high prediction accuracy [9]. However, portable handheld sensors combined with ML technology remain less investigated. Given these considerations, it is quite meaningful to integrate ML-driven strategies into smartphone-based platforms for rapid on-site detection of targets.

Mercury ion (Hg2+) is a highly toxic heavy metal cation that is readily absorbed by the human gastrointestinal tract and can pass through the blood–brain barrier, causing damage to the central nervous system [10–12]. A range of neurological problems can result from Hg2+ toxicity, including cognitive and motor impairments, vision and hearing loss [13–15]. These issues stem from the irreversible inhibition of enzymes caused by the binding of Hg2+ to amide, sulfhydryl, carboxyl, and amine groups [16]. On the other hand, glutathione (GSH), the most abundant non-protein thiol in animal cells, plays an essential role in controlling the redox state of the biological internal environment [17]. Abnormal levels of GSH in human body signal multiple diseases, including diabetes, AIDS, stroke, epilepsy, and cancer [18]. To date, continuous efforts have been made to detect Hg2+ and GSH. However, in-field determination of Hg2+ and GSH remains a daunting challenge as it usually requires expensive laboratory-based instruments and the professional operation by well-trained personnel. Therefore, the development of low-cost and user-friendly highly integrated sensors for quantification of Hg2+ and GSH is of great significance.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been promoted as a rising star in sensing applications owing to their unique merits such as excellent luminescent properties, easy synthesis, and convenient decoration [19–22]. However, most of them are single-emission intensity modulated, which limits the detection accuracy due to multiple factors such as background noise, instrumental drift, and concentration variance of probes [23]. Ratiometric fluorescence (FL) CDs possess outstanding self-calibration capabilities by analyzing intensity ratios of different emission peaks, thereby improving the reliability and accuracy of detection [24]. In this regard, there is an urgent need to combine ratiometric FL CDs with portable handheld devices for rapid visual quantification of targets.

Herein, we synthesize D-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method using neutral red and aloe tablets. As-prepared D-CDs present intrinsic FL ratio variations upon Hg2+ introduction, accompanied by a successive FL transition from orange to green. Subsequent addition of GSH restores the FL of D-CDs from green to orange, achieving dual-response analysis. To quantitatively read out the FL signals, we employ the ML method to construct an automated handheld sensor that integrates Hg2+-mediated ratiometric FL CDs, a 3D-printed device, and a smartphone with a self-programming program (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the automated sensor is successfully applied for GSH detection in real samples with a detection limit of 1.84 μM. Our study exhibits the following advantages: (1) The portable FL handheld device is complemented with a machine learning method, which reduces the errors of the artificial selection area and further improves the detection accuracy and efficiency. (2) This system utilizes a smartphone with a self-designed applet as a mobile terminal, enabling users to conveniently image samples and receive results instantly. (3) The highly integrated design offers a fast, portable, and easy-to-handle platform for high-throughput analysis of samples, making it particularly suitable for use in resource-limited environments and home healthcare settings. (4) Ratiometric FL D-CDs responding to Hg2+ and GSH are prepared without the physical mixing and nanohybrid strategies, avoiding time-consuming and cumbersome procedures. This study aims to leverage the irreplaceable advantages of machine learning in automatic recognition and data analysis to develop user-friendly automated sensors. To our knowledge, this is the first time that a ML-guided handheld sensor based on ratiometric FL CDs is constructed for GSH detection. This will provide a valuable guidance for the rational design of portable intelligent sensors in the future.
The materials, apparatus, measurement of quantum yield, cytotoxicity assays, sample treatments, and detection experiments in real samples are all listed in Supplementary Materials.

Fresh aloe leaves were washed, sliced, and dried to get aloe tablets. Aloe tablets (1.6 g), neutral red (0.02 g), and ultrapure water (30 mL) were sealed in a hydrothermal reactor at 180 ℃ for 2 h. The crude products were centrifuged using a high-speed centrifuge (3000 rpm, 15 min) and the supernatant was filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. Next, the mixture solution was dialyzed for 24 h to acquire purified D-CDs solution, which was then freeze-dried to obtain solid-state D-CDs.

In a typical route, 50 μL D-CDs solution was diluted with 500 μL PBS buffer (pH 7.0), and incubated with different contents of Hg2+ for 20 s. To detect GSH, different concentrations of GSH were dispersed into D-CDs@Hg2+ and incubated for 20 s. The FL spectra were instantly collected upon 400 nm excitation at room temperature.

The analytical platform, which comprises a 3D-printed accessory with optical components, ratiometric FL CDs, and a smartphone with a self-designed WeChat program, enables perform real-time detection. The independently designed 3D-printed optical accessory is produced using an It-600 printer (Fig. 1 ), which provides a stable environment to avoid interference from external lighting and camera flashing. As-developed ratiometric FL CDs have the ability to visualize targets. The proposed WeChat mini-program is deployed on both Android and Apple iOS operating systems on smartphones. The system has two major functions of signal capture and data analysis, which consists of ML-YOLOv3 algorithm, image-processing system, and color analyzing program. Once an image is captured, the system can intelligently recognize the FL regions, extract the RGB values, establish a linear relationship, and predict the sample concentration.

Selection of suitable precursors and introduction of heteroatoms play a key role in the synthesis of long-wavelength FL CDs. Currently, the strategy of preparing CDs through the combination of multiple precursors with different properties is widely used to develop high-performance FL CDs [25]. Based on this consideration, we use neutral red and aloe tablets as the raw materials to synthesize D-CDs. Neutral red possesses a larger conjugated structure, which is favorable for deep cross-linking carbonization under high temperature, ensuring the formation of large conjugated systems [26]. In addition, the amino groups of neutral red provide potential nitrogen-doping and hydrophilic functional groups, which contribute to the good water solubility and high quantum yield of D-CDs. At the same time, aloe tablets are rich in numerous active ingredients, including sugars, acids, cellulose, and anthraquinone compounds, which can not only participate in nucleation or surface functionalization modifications, but also efficiently improve the quantum yield of D-CDs [27]. During the heating process, two precursors undergo four stages of dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation to form D-CDs [28,29]. Notably, CDs-based ratiometric FL probes with dual- or multi-emitting properties are generally constructed by physical mixing and nanohybrid strategies. It is well known that these methods involve combining two types of luminescent units through covalent or non-covalent interactions, which necessitates the use of expensive instruments and complex procedures. In this study, dual-emission CDs are synthesized using appropriate raw materials, which can directly serve as ratiometric FL probes without the introduction of additional luminescent units as reference or response signals. This method is simple, cost-effective, and easy to control, making it ideal for sensing applications.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) reveal the morphology of D-CDs. Fig. 2 A indicates that D-CDs are nearly spherical and highly dispersed with an average particle diameter of 1.7 ± 0.3 nm. Moreover, D-CDs present a crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is attributable to the (102) facet of graphite (Fig. 2B). AFM images (Fig. 2C and D) show that D-CDs exhibit excellent dispersion and have a mean height of approximately 2.7 nm, which is in great agreement with TEM results.

The composition and chemical state of D-CDs can be analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2E, the full spectrum scanning of D-CDs shows three apparent peaks, which correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s respectively. The C1s spectrum of D-CDs (Fig. 2F) indicates the presence of C = C/C − C (284.7 eV), C − O/C − N (285.9 eV), and O = C − O (288.1 eV). The N1s spectrum of D-CDs (Fig. 2G) can be separated into two peaks: C − N (399.5 eV) and N − H (401.3 eV). The peaks at 532.0 eV and 533.3 eV in the O1s spectrum of D-CDs (Fig. 2H) are assigned to C = O and C − OH, respectively. Moreover, the functional groups of D-CDs are determined by Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) in Fig. 2I. A broad band located at 3202 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O − H. The characteristic absorption band at 857 cm−1 matches with the bending vibration of N − H. The absorption bands at 2870 cm−1 and 1423 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of = C − H, respectively. The peak at 1601 cm−1 is identified as the stretching vibration of C = O, and the peak at 863 cm−1 reveals the presence of C − O [30]. The results of XPS and FTIR show that D-CDs contain functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which form numerous emission traps that dominate the FL properties of D-CDs. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) displays a distinct diffraction peak at 22°, further confirming the crystalline structure of D-CDs.

The absorption and FL spectra of D-CDs are studied. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there exist two sharp peaks at 209 nm and 275 nm, which are associated with the π − π* transition of C = C in the core of D-CDs and the n − σ* transition of O − H/N − H on the surface of D-CDs, respectively. The absorption peak at 520 nm indicates the existence of C = O bond on the surface of D-CDs [31,32]. By altering the excitation wavelength from 370 nm to 450 nm with 10 nm intervals, the FL emission of D-CDs gradually shifts to higher wavelengths (Fig. 3B). Interestingly, the D-CDs show dual-emission behavior at 550 nm and 616 nm when excited at 400 nm. The excitation-independent emission originates from the presence of surface state and the excitation-dependent emission is associated with the conjugated aromatic π systems [33,34,35]. Furthermore, we investigate the optical stability of D-CDs. It is noticeable that no significant changes in FL intensity of D-CDs are observed under continuous irradiation (up to 30 min) (Fig. S2A), wide range of NaCl concentrations (up to 1.0 M) (Fig. S2B), and long-term storage conditions (up to 20 days) (Fig. S2C). These prominent properties lay the foundation for real-time and on-site sensing of D-CDs in practical applications. The relative quantum yield of D-CDs is calculated to be 23.8 % using Rhodamine B as a reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing of Hg2+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral selectivity is evaluated by comparing the FL responses of D-CDs to other substances under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S3, only Hg2+ induces a distinct change of the FL ratio (I616/I550), while other species show little or negligible effects. Taking into account the complexity of the real situation, we also explore the influence of competitive analytes on the ratiometric detection of Hg2+, and the results show that the FL responses are not affected even if the interfering substances coexist. It is obvious that the FL spectra of D-CDs show regular ratio changes with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, which is manifested in gradual decrease of FL intensity at 616 nm and corresponding increase of FL intensity at 550 nm (Fig. 3C). Functional relationship between I616/I550 and Hg2+ concentration is defined by the equation y = 0.4748 + 1.0851/(1 + exp((x − 90.3762)/dx)) (Fig. 3D), in which a good linear relationship is obtained when Hg2+ concentration is in the range of 30 − 150 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 20.95 nM based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Above results show that D-CDs are highly selective and sensitive FL probes for detection of Hg2+. By converting the titration spectra curve of Hg2+ into Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram, we note that there is a clear shift in color coordinates from orange-yellow, yellow to yellow-green, implying that the developed D-CDs have the potential to be applied for visual quantification of Hg2+ under a UV lamp (Fig. S4).

Prior to testing the sensing potential of D-CDs@Hg2+, the stability and toxicity of D-CDs@Hg2+ are investigated. The MTT assay is used to measure the toxicity of D-CDs@Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S5A, these cells still maintain satisfactory viability after incubation with different concentrations of D-CDs@Hg2+. In addition, the FL ratio (I616/I550) of D-CDs@Hg2+ remains nearly stable after storage for 5 days in Fig. S5B. These results indicate that D-CDs@Hg2+ possess long-term stability and low cytotoxicity, which facilitates their wide application in biological fields. In view of the above results, we test the selectivity of D-CDs@Hg2+. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the FL ratio (I616/I550) of D-CDs@Hg2+ significantly increases after the introduction of GSH, while other substances (up to 400 μM) have no effects. With the increase of GSH concentration in the range of 0 − 400 μM, the FL intensity of D-CDs@Hg2+ system at 616 nm regularly enhances, whereas the FL intensity at 550 nm greatly decreases in Fig. 3E. Fig. 3F shows the linear relationship between I616/I550 and GSH concentration (20 − 400 μM) with the corresponding linear equation y = 0.5461 + 0.0020x, where x and y refer to the concentration of GSH and the FL ratio. And the LOD is calculated to be 76.47 nM based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Above results confirm that the Hg2+-mediated D-CDs system possesses a high specificity toward GSH over competitive analytes and can be utilized as a ratiometric FL probe for tracking GSH concentration. Moreover, a color transition from yellow-green, yellow to orange-yellow is observed in the CIE diagram, which is in good agreement with GSH titration spectra in the D-CDs@Hg2+ system (Fig. S7). These results suggest the feasibility of D-CDs@Hg2+ for visual detection of GSH.

The reaction mechanism of D-CDs to Hg2+ and GSH is further discussed. It can be obviously observed that the zeta potential of D-CDs (Fig. S8A) increases from − 5.96 mV to + 1.19 mV after addition of Hg2+, but turns to − 5.98 mV in the presence of GSH, which is attributed to the consumption of negative groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups after D-CDs combine with Hg2+, while introduction of GSH releases the consumed negative groups of D-CDs. Furthermore, a new UV absorption peak appears at 330 nm as the Hg2+ concentration increases (Fig. S8B), indicating that D-CDs can react with Hg2+ to form a new complex, and the absorption peak gradually weakens when GSH is added into the reaction system (Fig. S8C), proving that the complex disappears. Accordingly, we infer that the functional groups on the surface of D-CDs interact with Hg2+ to form D-CDs@Hg2+ complexes, thus changing the FL of D-CDs, while subsequent addition of GSH results in stronger Hg2+@GSH complexes and restores the FL of D-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ml-assisted handheld sensor for on-site testing
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, most of the handheld quantitation systems adopt a smartphone as the signal detector to acquire images, but still rely on computer-based central facilities for data processing. Specifically, high-quality images are captured using the smartphone camera. These images are further transferred to a computer, and the uniform areas of these images are extracted RGB values. Then these data are processed through software programs to establish an optimal linear relationship. Obviously, this operation is time-consuming and requires user intervention, which is hard to meet on-site and real-time monitoring of a large number of samples.

To improve the detection efficiency and accuracy, we employ ML technology to develop a user-friendly intelligent system. The handheld system for quantifying analytes can acquire images, automatically identify region of interest (reaction zone), as well as output quantitative concentration values through custom-designed built-in program on a smartphone. The ML-driven program based on the YOLOv3 algorithm has two main functions of signal capture and data analysis. The model is trained with ML algorithms to gain the ability of locating FL images rapidly, which is more accurate and efficient than manual processing. A total of 570 FL images are collected as the dataset, of which 513 are randomly selected as the training dataset and the rest as the test dataset. YOLOv3 model implements both the target segmentation and category recognition tasks through a black box model, which means that the model can quickly and accurately output information on the position and size of the FL region after inputting photos with different angles, numbers, and FL colors. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 6 , when FL photographs are used as input, the model is first resized by the algorithm to 416*416, and given that pixels can be represented by RGB, the final input value is 416*416*3-dimensional data. These data are mapped to a 13*13, 26*26, 52*52 grid to perform predictions based on 3 priori boxes, accumulating 10,647 predictions, where each prediction includes 4 + 1 + n dimensions (4 for dimensional and positional information, 1 for confidence and n for category). In the detection layer, the YOLOv3 algorithm employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) model to implement end-to-end target detection. In addition, the introduction of a residual network optimizes the over-deep neural network and avoids gradient disappearance and gradient explosion, significantly improving training efficiency without compromising accuracy. The final aim of the YOLOv3 classification system is to identify the FL regions of all pictures in the test set, without identifying other items. True positives (TP) are defined as the number of photos that correctly identify the FL regions. False positives (FP) are defined as the number of photos that misidentify other objects as the FL regions. False negatives (FN) are defined as the number of unrecognized target images. Precision and recall denote the ratio of TP among all images and FL region identified, respectively, as expressed by Eqs. (1)−(2). The F1 score denotes a weighted average of model accuracy and recall given by Eq. (3).
(1)
P r e c i s i o n =
TP
TP + F P
(2)
R e c a l l =
TP
TP + F N
(3)
F 1 =
2 × P r e c i s i o n × R e c a l l
Precision + R e c a l l

Table S1 shows that the precision and recall of YOLOv3 are 96.5 % and 98.2 %, respectively, and the calculated F1 score is 97.3 %, which demonstrates the proposed ML algorithm-based AI model can accurately identify and locate the target FL region.

The utilization of WeChat mini-program is illustrated in Fig. 7 . Two main modules on the home screen represent real-time shooting and image uploading modes. Upon selecting the image uploading mode, users can click on the “fitting” option to generate a standard curve. Once the images are imported into the self-programming application, they are automatically processed, and the FL region is identified using the YOLOv3 algorithm. The RGB values of the identified regions are then fitted to the corresponding concentration. Subsequently, users can navigate to the secondary interface to perform real-time quantification of unknown sample concentrations utilizing the “calculate” module. As shown in Fig. S10, a linear relationship between (B + G)/R and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0 − 200 μM is obtained, and the calculated LOD is 0.82 µM. Meanwhile, the relationships between GSH concentrations (ranging from 0 to 400 μM) and various signal parameters (R, G, B, R/G, R/B, G/B, (R + G)/B, (R + B)/G, and (B + G)/R) are constructed (Fig. S11). The results indicate that the linear correlation between (B + G)/R and GSH concentration satisfies the requirements of optimal correlation, broad linear range, and low detection limit (1.84 μM), aligning well with the FL spectroscopic data. Furthermore, different linear fitting algorithms (Least Squares, Ridge, Lasso, Lasso Lars, Bayesian Ridge, and SVM) are examined to select the optimal fit. As can be seen in Fig. S12, the best algorithm is Least Squares. The above results suggest that the custom-built WeChat mini-program enables rapid and accurate real-time quantification of GSH concentration through (B + G)/R values. In addition, the “self-defined” button, located in the middle of interface, allows users to customize the objective function according to their specific requirements, enhancing its practicability of on-site quantification.

When testing unknown samples, the user operation is to drop the real samples into tubes, insert the tubes into the sample slots, and start the Wechat program to acquire an image 20 s after sample introduction. The overall process takes less than 30 s, including the response time of D-CDs@Hg2+ and GSH (20 s), along with the image acquisition and data processing time (less than 10 s). This demonstrates that the automated handheld sensor can be used for ultrafast detection of GSH on the spot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicality of ML-driven smartphone-based handheld sensors, we perform the standard recovery experiments in complex samples. As shown in Tables S2–S3, the recoveries for Hg2+ in these samples are in the range of 96.54–109.98 %, and low relative standard deviations are in the range of 2.05 − 4.84 %. The recoveries for GSH in these samples are in the range of 89.47–110.22 %, and low relative standard deviations are in the range of 1.23 − 5.02 %. These results confirm the proposed method has satisfactory repeatability and reliability for detecting Hg2+ and GSH. Meanwhile, the collected samples are also subjected to FL analysis as a reference. The data show a good agreement in quantitation results between the proposed method and FL analysis, proving that the ML-assisted smartphone-based handheld sensing method is a reliable method for on-site detection of Hg2+ and GSH and the automated sensor holds a great potential in various sensing applications.

The automated platform integrates ratiometric FL D-CDs, a 3D-printed device, and a ML-assisted intelligent image-processing system, which provides the end-user with a self-monitoring device suitable for point-of-care diagnostics. Compared to existing FL sensors, the proposed sensor is based on three key innovations (Tables S4–S5): (1) Ingenious construction of ML-assisted handheld sensing platform based on ratiometric FL CDs improves the accuracy and reliability of detection. (2) The self-developed WeChat applet serves as a result-processing terminal, which allows unskilled personnel to perform assays, extending its potential use in home analysis. (3) The automated platform achieves high-throughput detection of actual samples, which greatly improves the testing efficiency and facilitates practical applications. To our knowledge, a ML-assisted smartphone-integrated handheld strategy is first proposed for sample-to-answer detection. We anticipate that it will provide a meaningful guideline for rapid on-site chemical analysis and further prompt the research for the portable intelligent handheld sensors.

In this work, dual-emission CDs are synthesized through hydrothermal treatment using neutral red and aloe tablets, which can successively respond to Hg2+ and GSH accompanied by significant color changes. To meet the requirements of on-site monitoring, we develop a portable intelligent handheld detection system with ultrafast response and high accuracy that uses a smartphone as a result-processing terminal in the field. It is worth noting that the ML algorithm is incorporated into a custom-built WeChat mini-program installed on a smartphone to automatically locate, identify, and quantify. Users can easily obtain the test results of unknown samples by simply operating their smartphones. We anticipate that this research can provide a foundation and inspire new ideas for intelligent analysis in the fields of environmental protection and food safety.

Jianyang Feng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Data curation. Lihong Shi: Validation, Project administration. Dan Chang: Validation, Investigation. Chuan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090 and 21874087).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.151839.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots coupled with morin-Al3+: Cleverly design an integrated sensing platform for ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted visual detection of fluoride ion

Gong, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots coupled with morin-Al3+: Cleverly design an integrated sensing platform for ratiometric optical dual-mode and smartphone-assisted visual detection of fluoride ion, J. Hazard. Mater. 439 (2022) 129596−129610, https://doi. org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2022.129596.
